source_paper_title: Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Srikant_2025, title={Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Srikant, R.}, year={2025}, month=oct }
context:
Section 5 (Conclusions), page 19.

Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt{n})\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper. The Edgeworth expansion for scalar functions of Markov chains in (Kontoyiannis and Meyn 2003) suggests that the \(\log n\) could possibly be eliminated for Markov chains as well.
title: Improve martingale and Markov chain CLT Wasserstein convergence rate to the optimal 1/sqrt(n) rate
background_latex:
Let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\) be a \(d\)-dimensional martingale difference sequence with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\), i.e., \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-measurable, \(\mathbb E[\|m_k\|]<\infty\), and \(\mathbb E[m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]=0\) for all \(k\ge 1\). Define the partial sums \(S_n:=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) and the normalized sum \(W_n:=S_n/\sqrt n\).

Let \(d_W\) denote the 1-Wasserstein distance between \(d\)-dimensional random vectors \(X,Y\):
\[
 d_W(X,Y):=\sup_{h\in \mathrm{Lip}_1}\,\mathbb E[h(X)-h(Y)],\qquad \mathrm{Lip}_1:=\{h:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R: |h(x)-h(y)|\le \|x-y\|\}.
\]
Let \(\Sigma_\infty\succ 0\) be a target (asymptotic) covariance matrix, and let \(Z\sim \mathcal N(0,I_d)\). The paper establishes non-asymptotic bounds of order \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) (and more generally \(O(n^{-\beta/2}/(1-\beta))\) for \(\beta\in(0,1)\)) for \(d_W(W_n,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z)\) under moment and conditional covariance assumptions.

For an ergodic Markov chain \(\{X_k\}_{k\ge 0}\) (finite or general state space) with stationary distribution \(\pi\) and a reward function \(r:S\to\mathbb R^d\) with stationary mean \(\bar r:=\mathbb E_{\pi}[r(X)]\), define
\[
U_n:=\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_{k=1}^n (r(X_{k-1})-\bar r).
\]
Under standard ergodicity/drift conditions, Poisson's equation yields a decomposition of \(U_n\) into a martingale term plus a telescoping remainder, so rates for the martingale CLT translate into rates for the Markov chain CLT in Wasserstein distance.
problem_definition_latex:
Find additional conditions (beyond those used to prove an \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) Wasserstein bound) under which the martingale and corresponding Markov chain central limit theorems admit the sharper rate
\[
 d_W(W_n,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z)=O\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\right)
\]
(and hence similarly \(d_W(U_n,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z)=O(1/\sqrt n)\) via the Poisson-equation reduction), i.e., eliminate the extra \(\log n\) factor present in the paper's \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate.
latex:

\]
Under standard ergodicity/drift conditions, Poisson's equation yields a decomposition of \(U_n\) into a martingale term plus a telescoping remainder, so rates for the martingale CLT translate into rates for the Markov chain CLT in Wasserstein distance.

\[
 d_W(W_n,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z)=O\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\right)

subject_classification: probability theory (non-asymptotic central limit theorems for dependent processes)
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about improving an upper bound (removing a logarithmic factor) rather than refuting a conjectured inequality on a finite instance class. There is no clearly specified finite search space where automated testing could certify optimality or produce decisive counterexamples.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate proofs/conditions are not naturally representable as objects with an automatic fitness function. While one could numerically test rates for specific martingales/Markov chains, such experiments would not automatically verify a general theorem or identify the right additional hypotheses.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
